data.source: Online survey of American participants recruited via Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk)
data.wave_or_subset: Three studies total. Study 1 conducted March 19–20, 2020 (cross-sectional; 346 analyzed from 399 recruited) with a longitudinal subset that also completed related items in October 2019 (n=193). Later studies included a one-week longitudinal follow-up (T1 to T2) for invariance and predictive analyses.
data.sample_size: 1,195 participants across three studies; Study 1: 399 recruited (346 analyzed), with a longitudinal subset of 193 pre/post participants.
data.notes: Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale has two 5-item subscales (symbolic and realistic). Reported reliabilities include symbolic threat α=0.86 and realistic threat α=0.73. Additional measures included: adherence to CDC guidelines (1–5; reliability=0.68), support for public health initiatives (1–7; reliability=0.89), and social identity-affirming behaviors (1–5; reliability=0.80). Analyses included both cross-sectional and short-term longitudinal designs (approximately one-week interval), and a pre-pandemic comparison for a subset (Oct 2019).

method.description: The paper develops and validates a brief 10-item Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale capturing symbolic and realistic threat perceptions. Using MTurk samples, the authors conducted exploratory (PCA) and confirmatory (CFA) factor analyses to establish a two-factor structure, tested measurement invariance across time, and used structural equation modeling to examine criterion validity with psychological distress, well-being, public health compliance, and identity-affirming behaviors, both cross-sectionally and longitudinally.
steps: Recruit U.S. participants via MTurk and administer online surveys at specified time points (including pre-pandemic and during-pandemic, and a one-week follow-up in later studies).

Administer the 10-item Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale (5 symbolic, 5 realistic) along with convergent/divergent validity measures (e.g., need for cognitive closure, belief in a dangerous world, Schwartz values, TIPI).

Measure criterion outcomes including anxiety, COVID-19 impact intrusion and avoidance, positive/negative affect, life satisfaction, adherence to CDC guidelines, support for public health initiatives, and social identity-affirming behaviors.
Conduct PCA to explore factor structure, followed by CFA to test a two-factor model versus a single-factor model; assess reliability.
Evaluate convergent and divergent validity via correlations with trait measures and values.
Use SEM to test associations between symbolic/realistic threat and psychological/behavioral outcomes cross-sectionally and prospectively (T1 threats predicting T2 outcomes).
Test longitudinal measurement invariance (configural, metric, scalar) and latent mean equivalence across a one-week interval.
Report effect estimates, model fit indices, and variance explained; conduct supplementary analyses (e.g., political ideology) as applicable.
method.models: Principal Component Analysis (PCA); Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA); Longitudinal measurement invariance testing (configural, metric, scalar, latent means); Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) for criterion validity.
method.outcome_variable: Multiple outcomes: general anxiety; COVID-19 impact intrusion and avoidance; negative and positive affect; life satisfaction; adherence to CDC guidelines; support for public health initiatives; social identity-affirming behaviors.
method.independent_variables: Perceived symbolic threat and perceived realistic threat (latent factors from the Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale).
method.control_variables: Threat factors covaried; in longitudinal analyses, associations were tested from T1 threats to T2 outcomes; pre-pandemic anxiety was controlled when predicting pandemic anxiety in the subset with Oct 2019 data; supplementary analyses considered political ideology.

results.summary: Across three studies (total N=1,195), the Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale exhibited a robust two-factor structure distinguishing symbolic and realistic threats. PCA indicated two factors explaining substantial variance; CFA supported the two-factor model with acceptable fit and superiority over a single-factor model. Both threats were associated with greater psychological distress (anxiety, intrusion), but they diverged on public health behaviors: realistic threat predicted higher adherence to CDC guidelines and greater support for public health initiatives, whereas symbolic threat predicted lower adherence/support and more social identity-affirming behaviors. Longitudinal analyses showed strong measurement invariance over a one-week interval and that T1 threats predicted T2 outcomes. Realistic threat was consistently linked to increased anxiety and decreased life satisfaction, while symbolic threat related to avoidance and intrusion and, unexpectedly, to positive affect via identity-affirming behaviors.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Avoidance (Impact of Event Scale) associated with symbolic threat
Intrusion (Impact of Event Scale) associated with symbolic threat
General anxiety associated with realistic threat
Intrusion (Impact of Event Scale) associated with realistic threat
General anxiety during pandemic (controlling pre-pandemic anxiety) associated with realistic threat
